title: What probability is, and the favorable-over-total ratio
deck: Probability is a number between 0 and 1 that measures how likely an event is to occur. For simple events with equally-likely outcomes, the math reduces to one ratio: favorable outcomes over total outcomes.
objectives: Compute the probability of a simple event as the ratio of favorable outcomes to total outcomes, and express the answer as a fraction in lowest terms, a decimal, or a percent.

content.text: Open any newspaper and probability statements are everywhere. The weather app says “70% chance of rain tomorrow.” A pharmaceutical company reports that a drug is “95% effective at preventing infection.” A casino game advertises a “1-in-38 chance of hitting your number.” A friend offers to settle an argument by flipping a coin, dismissing the result in advance as “50/50.” Three different scenarios with three very different stakes, but underneath each statement is the same mathematical object: a number between zero and one that measures how likely something is to happen.

This chapter develops the machinery to read those statements precisely and to compute them for ourselves. We begin in this section with the simplest case, in which an experiment has a small finite number of equally-likely outcomes. From this starting point the rest of the chapter generalizes: Section 6.2 handles the case in which the sample space is too large to list, Section 6.3 combines two events with OR, Section 6.4 distinguishes ordered selections from unordered ones, Section 6.5 turns probabilities into a single long-run average called the expected value, and Section 6.6 compares predictions from the math with results from the data.
6.1.1  The sample space and the probability ratio

An experiment is any process whose outcome cannot be predicted with certainty: rolling a die, drawing a card from a shuffled deck, flipping a coin, spinning a wheel, or even (with a longer view) recording tomorrow's high temperature. The set of all possible outcomes of an experiment is called the sample space. For a single fair six-sided die, the sample space is the set S = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}; for a single coin flip it is {H, T}; for drawing one card from a deck it is the set of all 52 named cards. An event is any subset of the sample space, that is, any collection of outcomes we wish to count as “yes.”

content.caption: Figure 6.1. The probability scale runs from 0 (the event cannot happen) to 1 (the event is guaranteed). Every probability statement that arises in this chapter sits somewhere on this number line.

content.title: Simple probability
content.content:
The **probability** of an event is the ratio of **favorable outcomes** to **total possible outcomes** in the sample space, assuming each outcome is equally likely:

**P(event) = favorable outcomes / total outcomes**

Every probability is a number between **0** (impossible) and **1** (certain). Reduce the fraction to lowest terms when ALEKS asks for an exact answer.

content.text: Two boundary values deserve special attention. When the event contains no outcomes at all (it is the empty set), the probability is exactly 0; the event is impossible. When the event contains every outcome in the sample space, the probability is exactly 1; the event is certain. Every other probability sits strictly between these two extremes.

Margin noteThe convention that probabilities live in [0, 1] is one of the three Kolmogorov axioms, laid down in 1933, that form the modern foundation of the subject. Before Kolmogorov, the field had been worked on for centuries (Cardano, Pascal, Fermat, Bernoulli, Laplace) without a fully rigorous setup.

The whole calculation reduces to two counts. We must count the outcomes in the sample space, then count the outcomes inside the event of interest, and divide. The arithmetic is elementary; the work, in any nontrivial probability problem, is in setting up the counts correctly. Three habits make a difference. First, list the sample space explicitly when it is small (six faces of a die, fifty-two cards in a deck), so that we know exactly what we are dividing by. Second, identify the event in plain English and translate it into a subset before counting. Third, reduce the resulting fraction to lowest terms unless the problem specifies otherwise.
The probability of an event is a count divided by a count. Get the two counts right and the math falls out automatically.

Common mistakeCounting outcomes that are not equally likely. The formula P(A) = n(A) / n(S) assumes that every outcome in S has the same chance of occurring. If a coin is weighted so heads is twice as likely as tails, then we cannot say P(H) = 1/2 just because the sample space has two elements. When outcomes are unequal, we weight each outcome by its individual probability (this is exactly the idea behind expected value in Section 6.5).

Example 6.1. The ALEKS letter card.

A box contains eight cards labeled P, Q, R, S, T, U, V, and W. One card will be chosen at random. Find the probability of choosing a card whose letter is between P and T inclusive.

Solution.

Step 1. Identify the sample space. The eight cards form the sample space S = {P, Q, R, S, T, U, V, W}, so n(S) = 8.

Step 2. Identify the event. “A letter from P to T” means the subset {P, Q, R, S, T}, which has five elements; so n(A) = 5.

Step 3. Form the ratio.

P(A) = n(A) / n(S) = 5 / 8

Step 4. Reduce if possible. The numerator 5 is prime and shares no factor greater than 1 with the denominator 8 = 2·2·2, so the fraction is already in lowest terms.

The probability that a random card lies in the interval P–T is 5/8, or in decimal form 0.625, which on the probability scale of Figure 6.1 sits between “even chance” and “likely.”

In the wildWeather forecasters report probabilities of precipitation with the same favorable-over-total interpretation, applied to outcomes the model considers equally consistent with the available atmospheric data. A “70 percent chance of rain” means that of the ensemble of plausible weather trajectories the model has simulated, roughly 70 percent end with measurable rainfall at the forecast location during the forecast window. Probability shows up in modern meteorology, in epidemiology, in actuarial science, in artificial intelligence, in genomics, and in nearly every quantitative field where uncertainty is intrinsic to the question.

For every event A there is a companion event Ac, called the complement of A, that consists of every outcome in the sample space that is not in A. Since every outcome is either in A or in Ac and no outcome can be in both, the two probabilities must sum to one: P(A) + P(Ac) = 1. Rearranged, this gives the complement rule: P(Ac) = 1 − P(A). The rule is most useful when counting the “not” outcomes is easier than counting the “yes” outcomes. If a weather report says P(rain) = 0.7, then P(no rain) = 1 − 0.7 = 0.3, no further reasoning required.

content.problem: A fair six-sided die is rolled once. What is the probability of rolling an even number? Write your answer as a fraction in lowest terms.
content.answer: 1/2. The sample space {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6} has six equally-likely outcomes; the event “even” is {2, 4, 6} with three outcomes. The probability is 3/6 = 1/2.

content.problem: A standard 52-card deck is shuffled and one card is drawn at random. What is the probability that it is a heart?
content.answer: 1/4. The deck has 13 hearts out of 52 cards, so the probability is 13/52 = 1/4.

content.problem: A bag contains 4 red marbles, 6 blue marbles, and 10 green marbles. One marble is drawn at random. What is the probability that it is blue?
content.answer: 3/10. The bag contains 4 + 6 + 10 = 20 marbles, of which 6 are blue. So the probability is 6/20 = 3/10.

content.problem:
Which of the following values cannot be the probability of any event?

  1. 0
  2. 1/3
  3. 1.4
  4. 0.95
content.answer: C. Probabilities live in [0, 1]. A value of 1.4 lies outside this range and so cannot represent the probability of any event.

content.problem: If P(A) = 0.65, what is P(Ac), the probability that A does not occur?

A raffle has 200 tickets sold, and the winning ticket is chosen at random from a drum that contains them all. You bought 15 tickets. What is the probability that you win? Write your answer as a fraction in lowest terms.
content.answer: 3/40. The probability is 15/200 = 3/40, after dividing numerator and denominator by their greatest common factor 5.

content.problem: A 52-card deck. What is the probability of drawing (a) a **heart**? (b) a **queen**? (c) a **face card** (jack, queen, king)?
A bag holds **8 red marbles, 5 blue, 3 green**. You draw one at random. Find P(red), P(blue), and P(not green). Verify P(green) + P(not green) = 1.

content.title: Simple probability from a die roll
content.problem: A fair six-sided die is rolled once. What is the probability of rolling a **4**? What is the probability of rolling an **even number**?
content.solution:
**Probability = favorable outcomes / total outcomes.**

Total outcomes for one die roll: 6 (the faces 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6).

**P(rolling a 4):** one favorable outcome (the face 4).

P = 1/6 ≈ **0.167** or about 16.7%.

**P(rolling an even number):** three favorable outcomes (2, 4, 6).

P = 3/6 = **1/2** = 0.5 = 50%.

Always reduce the fraction at the end (3/6 → 1/2).

content.title: Boundary values: impossible and certain events
content.problem: A standard deck has 52 cards. What is the probability of drawing **a card that is both red and a spade**? What is the probability of drawing **any card at all**?
content.solution:
**Boundary cases of probability.**

**P(red and spade):** Spades are black (hearts and diamonds are red). So there is no card that is *both* red and a spade.

Favorable outcomes: **0**. P = 0/52 = **0**. The event is **impossible**.

**P(drawing any card):** Every card in the deck is "a card." Favorable outcomes: 52. P = 52/52 = **1**. The event is **certain**.

*Probability is always between 0 and 1.* Any answer outside that range means you double-counted or set up the fraction backwards.

content.title: Summary
content.content: Simple probability is **favorable / total**, always a number between 0 (impossible) and 1 (certain). Both numerator and denominator must count the same kind of thing (cards from a deck, faces of a die, marbles from a bag). Reduce the fraction at the end.

content.title: Probability in everyday algorithms
content.content: Probability is hiding everywhere in your phone. Spotify Discover Weekly is ranking songs by *probability you'll like them*. Tinder is sorting profiles by *probability of a match*. Your camera roll's "For You" memory is selecting photos by *probability of being personally meaningful*. Every recommender system in tech is a probability machine. The math we're learning in this section is the foundation of how those systems work — they assign probabilities to outcomes and pick the highest-likelihood ones. You're learning to think the way these systems think.

content.text: Everything in the rest of the chapter is built on the favorable-over-total ratio of this section.
